sentences.base_language: Karim had been meaning to get back into shape for months and finally signed up for a gym membership.
sentences.target_language: كان كريمٌ يعتزم العودةَ إلى اللياقة البدنية منذ أشهرٍ، وأخيراً سجَّل اشتراكَه في الصالة الرياضية.
sentences.romanized: Kāna Karīmun yaʿtazimu l-ʿawdata ilā l-liyāqati l-badaniyyati mundhu ashurin, wa-akhīran sajjala shtirākahu fī ṣ-ṣālati r-riyāḍiyyah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**كان يعتزم** is the كان + مضارع past progressive/habitual construction, expressing that the intention had been ongoing — he had been meaning to; اعتزم is Form VIII of عزم (to resolve/intend), and the Form VIII pattern افتعل here conveys an inward, deliberate resolve as opposed to a casual wish.
**سجَّل اشتراكَه** uses Form II of سجَّل (to register/enrol), a doubly-weak Form II verb meaning to formally record; اشتراك is the masdar of Form VIII اشترك (to participate/subscribe), and اشتراكَه in the accusative as direct object of سجَّل forms an إضافة with the pronoun ـه, the standard MSA phrase for signing up for a membership.

sentences.base_language: On his first morning, he arrived early and felt a mix of motivation and slight intimidation.
sentences.target_language: في صباح يومه الأول، وصل مبكِّراً وشعر بخليطٍ من الدافعية وشيءٍ من الرهبة.
sentences.romanized: Fī ṣabāḥi yawmihi l-awwali, waṣala mubakkiran wa-shaʿara bi-khalīṭin min ad-dāfiʿiyyati wa-shayʾin min ar-rahbah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**صباح يومه الأول** is a three-element إضافة chain: صباح governs يوم in the genitive, يوم governs the pronoun suffix ـه, and الأول is a post-nominal adjective agreeing with يوم in the genitive — the morning of his first day; case propagation through the chain means each head noun is genitive to the one before it.
**شعر بـ** is the standard MSA construction for to feel [an emotion], where the preposition بـ introduces the nominal content of the feeling; خليطٍ من is an indefinite noun followed by من partitive, a common formal pattern for a mix of two contrasting nouns.

sentences.base_language: A friendly personal trainer at the reception offered to show him around the equipment.
sentences.target_language: عرض عليه مدرِّبٌ شخصيٌّ لطيفٌ عند مكتب الاستقبال أن يُرِيَه تجهيزاتِ الصالة.
sentences.romanized: ʿAraḍa ʿalayhi mudarribun shakhṣiyyun laṭīfun ʿinda maktabi l-istiqbāli an yuriyahu tajhīzāti ṣ-ṣālah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**أن يُرِيَه** uses lām al-taʿlīl implied within عرض + أن + مضارع منصوب (subjunctive); يُري is Form IV of رأى (أرى, to show/cause to see), a doubly-weak verb whose Form IV imperfect subjunctive drops the final vowel marker, yielding يُرِيَ; the ـه suffix is the direct object (him), while تجهيزاتِ الصالة is the second object of this ditransitive Form IV verb.
**تجهيزاتِ الصالة** is a sound feminine plural إضافة: تجهيزات (equipment, plural of تجهيز — masdar of Form II جهَّز, to equip) governs الصالة in the genitive; the construct pulls the definite article off تجهيزات itself while rendering the whole phrase definite through its genitive tail.

sentences.base_language: The trainer explained that consistency was more important than intensity, especially at the start.
sentences.target_language: أوضح المدرِّبُ أن الانتظامَ أهمُّ من الشدَّة، لا سيَّما في البداية.
sentences.romanized: Awḍaḥa l-mudarribu anna l-intiẓāma ahammu min ash-shiddati, lā siyyamā fī l-bidāyah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**أوضح** is Form IV of وضح (to be clear), meaning to clarify/explain; Form IV is causative here — to make something clear — and أوضح takes an أنَّ clause as its logical object, the standard MSA complement pattern for verbs of explanation and cognition.
**أهمُّ من** is the elative form (أفعل التفضيل) of هام/مهم (important), on the pattern أفعل — أهمّ — used comparatively with من; **لا سيَّما** is a fixed formal MSA phrase meaning especially/particularly, invariable in form and frequently found in formal written registers to single out a specific case from a general statement.

sentences.base_language: Karim started with twenty minutes on the treadmill, which felt manageable at first.
sentences.target_language: بدأ كريمٌ بعشرين دقيقةً على جهاز المشي، الذي بدا في وهلته الأولى في متناول اليد.
sentences.romanized: Badaʾa Karīmun bi-ʿishrīna daqīqatan ʿalā jihāzi l-mashyi, alladhī badā fī wahlatih l-ūlā fī mutanāwali l-yad.
sentences.grammarNote:
**جهاز المشي** is an إضافة: جهاز (device/apparatus) governs the masdar المشي (the walking, from مشى) in the genitive — the walking machine, the standard MSA term for treadmill; using a masdar in إضافة with a device noun is a productive formal Arabic naming convention.
**الذي بدا** is a relative clause introduced by the masculine singular relative pronoun الذي, agreeing with جهاز; **في متناول اليد** is a fixed idiom meaning within reach / manageable, literally in the reach of the hand, an إضافة chain (متناول + اليد) preceded by the preposition في.

sentences.base_language: By the tenth minute, his legs were heavy, his lungs were burning, and sweat was dripping down his face.
sentences.target_language: بحلول الدقيقة العاشرة، كانت ساقاه ثقيلتين ورئتاه تحترقان والعرقُ يتقاطر على وجهه.
sentences.romanized: Bi-ḥulūli d-daqīqati l-ʿāshirati, kānat sāqāhu thaqīlatayni wa-riʾtāhu taḥtariqāni wal-ʿarqu yataqāṭaru ʿalā wajhih.
sentences.grammarNote:
**ساقاه ثقيلتين / رئتاه تحترقان** demonstrate the full Arabic dual paradigm: ساقاه and رئتاه are dual nominatives with the possessive suffix ـه; ثقيلتين is the dual feminine indefinite accusative of ثقيل; تحترقان is the dual feminine imperfect of Form VIII احترق, carrying the obligatory ـانِ suffix for dual subjects in MSA.
**يتقاطر** is Form VI of قطر (to drip), on the pattern تفاعل; Form VI here conveys repeated, distributed dripping — drops falling one after another — more vivid than the simple Form I يقطر, capturing the continuous trickle of sweat rather than a single drop.

sentences.base_language: He slowed the machine to a walking pace, telling himself this still counted as progress.
sentences.target_language: خفَّض سرعةَ الجهاز إلى وتيرة المشي، مُحدِّثاً نفسَه بأن هذا لا يزال يُعدُّ تقدُّماً.
sentences.romanized: Khaffaḍa surʿata l-jihāzi ilā watīrati l-mashyi, muḥaddithān nafsahu bi-anna hādhā lā yazālu yuʿaddu taqaddumā.
sentences.grammarNote:
**خفَّض** is Form II of خفض (to lower/reduce), the intensive-causative form — to bring down deliberately; Form II is the standard verb for reducing speed or volume in MSA, and سرعةَ الجهاز as its direct object is an إضافة in the accusative.
**يُعدُّ** is the Form I passive imperfect of عدَّ (to count/consider), meaning it is considered/it counts as; the passive is formed by changing internal vowels (يُعَدُّ), and تقدُّماً is the accusative complement (tamyīz-like predicate) of the passive verb — a standard MSA construction for to be considered [something].

sentences.base_language: Next, the trainer showed him a series of strength exercises using dumbbells.
sentences.target_language: بعد ذلك، أراه المدرِّبُ سلسلةً من تمارين القوة باستخدام الأثقال اليدوية.
sentences.romanized: Baʿda dhālika, arāhu l-mudarribu silsilatan min tamārīni l-quwwati bi-stikhdāmi l-athqāli l-yadawiyyah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**أراه** is Form IV of رأى (أرى, to show/cause to see), a doubly-weak verb; the Form IV imperfect أُرِي loses its final weak radical in many inflected forms, and here أرى in the past tense takes two objects — the indirect object pronoun ـه (him) fused to the verb, and the direct object سلسلةً — making it a true ditransitive causative.
**باستخدام** uses بـ + the masdar of Form X استخدم (to use/employ); the Form X pattern استفعل here carries a sense of making use of available means — الأثقال اليدوية (hand-held weights, literally manual weights) — and بـ + masdar is the formal MSA construction for expressing instrument or method.

sentences.base_language: Karim's arms trembled on the last two repetitions of each set, which the trainer said was a good sign.
sentences.target_language: ارتجفت ذراعا كريمٍ في التكرارين الأخيرين من كل مجموعة، وهو ما أكَّد المدرِّبُ أنه علامةٌ جيدة.
sentences.romanized: Irtajafat dhirāʿā Karīmin fī t-takrārayni l-akhīrayni min kulli majmūʿatin, wa-huwa mā akkada l-mudarribu annahu ʿalāmatun jayyidah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**ارتجفت ذراعا كريمٍ** uses Form VIII of رجف (ارتجف, to tremble/shake), an intensive reflexive expressing involuntary trembling; ذراعا is the dual construct form of ذراع (arm), governing the genitive كريمٍ in إضافة — the two arms of Karim — and the verb takes the feminine singular agreement ارتجفت because dual non-human subjects in formal MSA may take feminine singular verb agreement in VSO order.
**التكرارين الأخيرين** shows full dual case agreement in the genitive: التكرارين is the dual genitive of التكرار (repetition, masdar of Form II كرَّر), and الأخيرين is its genitive dual adjective — the last two; both carry the dual genitive suffix ـين, and this complete adjectival agreement in case and number is obligatory in MSA.

sentences.base_language: After the session, every muscle in his body ached in a deep but not unpleasant way.
sentences.target_language: بعد انتهاء الجلسة، كانت كل عضلةٍ في جسده تؤلمه بطريقةٍ عميقةٍ ليست مزعجةً تماماً.
sentences.romanized: Baʿda ntihāʾi l-jalasati, kānat kullu ʿaḍalatin fī jasadihi tuʾlimuhu bi-ṭarīqatin ʿamīqatin laysat muzʿijatan tamāmā.
sentences.grammarNote:
**كانت... تؤلمه** is the كان + مضارع past progressive construction — was aching; كل عضلةٍ (every muscle) takes a singular indefinite genitive noun, which is the standard MSA rule: كل in the sense of every governs an indefinite singular genitive noun, even when the intended meaning is plural.
**ليست مزعجةً** introduces a negated nominal clause as a modifier; ليست is the feminine form of ليس (one of the كان sisters used for negation), negating the predicate مزعجةً — not being unpleasant — and the entire negated nominal clause functions as a descriptive qualification of the preceding طريقة, a sophisticated MSA embedding strategy.

sentences.base_language: He sat in the changing room for a few minutes, too tired to move but feeling genuinely proud.
sentences.target_language: جلس في غرفة تغيير الملابس دقائقَ معدودةً، من فرط التعب عاجزاً عن الحركة، غير أنه كان يشعر بفخرٍ حقيقي.
sentences.romanized: Jalasa fī ghurfati taghyīri l-malābisi daqāʾiqa maʿdūdatan, min farṭi t-taʿabi ʿājizān ʿani l-ḥarakati, ghayra annahu kāna yashʿuru bi-fakhrin ḥaqīqī.
literal: من فرط التعب عاجزاً عن الحركة
from excess the-fatigue unable of the-movement
غير أنه كان يشعر بفخرٍ حقيقي
except that-he was feeling with-pride genuine
sentences.grammarNote:
**غرفة تغيير الملابس** is a three-level إضافة: غرفة governs the masdar تغيير (changing, Form II of غيَّر) in the genitive, which in turn governs الملابس (the clothes) in the genitive — the room of the changing of clothes; stacking masdar إضافة chains is a hallmark of formal MSA nominal style.
**من فرط التعب عاجزاً** combines the idiomatic phrase من فرط + masdar (out of excess of / from too much) with the ḥāl participle عاجزاً (unable, active participle of عجز); عاجز takes عن + masdar (الحركة) as its complement, and the whole phrase functions as a simultaneous ḥāl explaining the condition in which he sat.

sentences.base_language: He drank a large bottle of water and ate a banana on a bench outside.
sentences.target_language: شرب قارورةً كبيرةً من الماء وتناول موزةً جالساً على مقعدٍ خارج الصالة.
sentences.romanized: Shariba qārūratan kabīratan min al-māʾi wa-tanāwala mawzatan jālisan ʿalā maqʿadin khārija ṣ-ṣālah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**تناول** is Form VI of نال (تناول, to eat/partake of), conveying deliberate, measured consumption rather than the simple Form I أكل (to eat); Form VI تفاعل carries a connotation of gradual, extended action, and تناول is the formal register verb of choice for eating in literary MSA.
**جالساً** is the Form I active participle of جلس (to sit), functioning as a ḥāl in the accusative — describing the simultaneous state of Karim while he ate; the ḥāl participle is indefinite and accusative, agreeing with the implicit subject هو of the main verb تناول, a standard MSA ḥāl construction.

sentences.base_language: Walking home, his legs felt heavy but his mood had lifted enormously.
sentences.target_language: وهو يسير إلى البيت كانت ساقاه ثقيلتين، غير أن مزاجَه كان قد تحسَّن تحسُّناً بالغاً.
sentences.romanized: Wa-huwa yasīru ilā l-bayti kānat sāqāhu thaqīlatayni, ghayra anna mizājahu kāna qad taḥassana taḥassunan bālighan.
sentences.grammarNote:
**وهو يسير** is a wāw al-ḥāl clause — a nominal sentence (pronoun + imperfect) expressing a simultaneous background circumstance — while he was walking; this ḥāl construction is the standard MSA way to frame a main clause with a co-occurring action or state, attaching to the subject of the main verb.
**كان قد تحسَّن تحسُّناً بالغاً** combines the past perfect (كان قد + perfect) with a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق): تحسَّن is Form V of حسن (to improve oneself/get better), and تحسُّناً بالغاً — the masdar of Form V in the accusative + the adjective بالغاً (great/far-reaching) — is the cognate accusative intensifying the verb, a formal rhetorical device in MSA.

sentences.base_language: That evening, his muscles began to stiffen and he had to sit down carefully.
sentences.target_language: في ذلك المساء، بدأت عضلاتُه تتصلَّب وأُرغم على الجلوس بحذرٍ شديد.
sentences.romanized: Fī dhālika l-masāʾi, badaʾat ʿaḍalātuhu tataṣallahu wa-urghima ʿalā l-julūsi bi-ḥadharin shadīd.
sentences.grammarNote:
**تتصلَّب** is Form V of صلب (تصلَّب, to become rigid/stiffen), the reflexive-resultative of Form II صلَّب; Form V تفعَّل typically denotes the reflexive or gradual acquisition of a quality — here, the muscles gradually stiffening of their own accord — making it more precise than the simple Form I verb.
**أُرغم على** is the Form IV passive (أُرغِم, to be compelled/forced) of رغم, taking the preposition على + masdar as complement; the passive voice here removes the agent (no one forced him — circumstances did), which is a stylistically motivated choice in MSA narrative to emphasise the involuntary nature of the action.

sentences.base_language: Despite the soreness, Karim felt a sense of determination he had not felt in years.
sentences.target_language: على الرغم من الوجع، أحسَّ كريمٌ بعزيمةٍ لم يُحِسَّ بها منذ سنواتٍ طويلة.
sentences.romanized: ʿAlā r-raghmi min al-wajaʿi, aḥassa Karīmun bi-ʿazīmatin lam yuḥissa bihā mundhu sanawātin ṭawīlah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**أحسَّ** is Form IV of حسَّ (أحسَّ, to feel/sense), a doubled root verb; the doubling (شدَّة) on the final radical is retained throughout the Form IV paradigm, and أحسَّ بـ is the formal MSA collocation for to feel/sense [an emotion], taking بـ + indefinite noun as complement.
**لم يُحِسَّ بها** uses لم + مضارع مجزوم (jussive) for past negation; the jussive of Form IV of a doubled verb requires gemination of the final consonant (يُحِسَّ), and the resumptive pronoun بها (with it) within the relative clause refers back to the indefinite antecedent عزيمة — a relative clause without a relative pronoun because the head noun is indefinite, a core MSA relative clause rule.

sentences.base_language: He downloaded a fitness app and set a goal of going to the gym three times a week.
sentences.target_language: حمَّل تطبيقاً للياقة البدنية وحدَّد هدفاً بالذهاب إلى الصالة ثلاثَ مراتٍ أسبوعياً.
sentences.romanized: Ḥammala taṭbīqan lil-liyāqati l-badaniyyati wa-ḥaddada hadafan bidh-dhahābi ilā ṣ-ṣālati thalātha marrātin usbūʿiyyā.
sentences.grammarNote:
**حمَّل** is Form II of حمل (to carry/load), extended in modern MSA to mean to download (to load onto a device); Form II's intensifying pattern transfers naturally to the digital register, and حمَّل is now the standard MSA verb for downloading files or applications.
**ثلاثَ مراتٍ** is a counted noun construction: ثلاث (three, in the accusative) governs مراتٍ (times, broken plural of مرَّة) in the genitive, with both words indefinite; for numbers three through ten in MSA, the number takes the opposite gender of the noun and the noun appears in the genitive plural — here مراتٍ is feminine plural in the genitive, and ثلاثَ is the masculine form, reflecting the polarity rule.

sentences.base_language: The session two days later was noticeably easier — his body had already begun to adapt.
sentences.target_language: كانت الجلسةُ التي أُجريَت بعد يومين أيسرَ بشكلٍ ملحوظٍ — إذ كان جسدُه قد أخذ يتأقلم بالفعل.
sentences.romanized: Kānat al-jalasatu llatī ujriyat baʿda yawmayni aysara bi-shaklin malḥūẓin — idh kāna jasaduhu qad akhadha yataʾaqlamu bil-fiʿl.
literal: إذ كان جسدُه قد أخذ يتأقلم
for was his-body had begun to-adapt
sentences.grammarNote:
**أُجريَت** is the Form IV passive perfect of جرى (أجرى, to conduct/carry out), a weak-final verb; the passive of Form IV of a final-weak verb contracts the ending to ـيَت in the feminine, and the passive construction removes the agent, emphasising the session itself rather than who organised it.
**أخذ يتأقلم** is the inceptive construction أخذ + imperfect (Form V تأقلم, to adapt/acclimatise, from Form II أقلم); أخذ + مضارع is one of the standard MSA inceptive periphrases meaning began to, and **تأقلم** (Form V of أقلم) conveys gradual self-adaptation to new conditions, the reflexive counterpart of أقلم (to acclimatise something).

sentences.base_language: The trainer greeted him by name and said his form had already improved considerably.
sentences.target_language: استقبله المدرِّبُ بالاسم وقال إن أسلوبَه قد تحسَّن كثيراً بالفعل.
sentences.romanized: Istaqbalahu l-mudarribu bil-ismi wa-qāla inna uslūbahu qad taḥassana kathīran bil-fiʿl.
sentences.grammarNote:
**استقبله بالاسم** uses Form X of قبل (استقبل, to receive/welcome), with the preposition بـ introducing the manner — by name; Form X استفعل here implies actively going to meet and receive, more formal and deliberate than the simple لقي or رحَّب بـ, and استقبل بالاسم is the standard MSA phrase for greeting someone by name.
**إنَّ** (here in the reduced form إن after قال) is the particle of emphasis and assertion that introduces the nominal sentence أسلوبَه قد تحسَّن; after قال (to say), MSA uses إنَّ rather than أنَّ because the reported speech is the direct object clause and إنَّ marks it as an asserted, direct assertion — a key distinction in MSA between indirect (أنَّ) and direct emphatic (إنَّ) speech-report clauses.

sentences.base_language: After a month of regular training, his energy and mood had transformed in ways people around him began to notice.
sentences.target_language: بعد شهرٍ من التدريب المنتظم، تحوَّلت طاقتُه ومزاجُه تحوُّلاً لفت انتباهَ من حوله.
sentences.romanized: Baʿda shahrin min at-tadrībi l-muntaẓami, taḥawwalat ṭāqatuhu wa-mizājuhu taḥawwulan lafata ntibāha man ḥawlah.
sentences.grammarNote:
**تحوَّل** is Form V of حال (تحوَّل, to transform/change), the reflexive of Form II حوَّل (to transform something); Form V tafaʿʿala marks an internally driven change, appropriate for describing how his energy and mood changed of their own accord through his sustained effort.
**تحوُّلاً لفت انتباهَ من حوله** uses the cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) — تحوُّل, the masdar of Form V, in the accusative — followed by an adjectival relative clause لفت انتباهَ من حوله (that attracted the attention of those around him); **من حوله** is a compound relative phrase (من + ظرف حوله) meaning those who are around him, a formal MSA construction used as an implicit relative pronoun phrase.

sentences.base_language: What had started as an obligation had quietly become something he genuinely looked forward to each week.
sentences.target_language: ما كان قد بدأ التزاماً تحوَّل في صمتٍ إلى شيءٍ ينتظره كريمٌ بصدقٍ كلَّ أسبوع.
sentences.romanized: Mā kāna qad badaʾa ltizāman taḥawwala fī ṣamtin ilā shayʾin yantaẓiruhu Karīmun bi-ṣidqin kulla usbūʿ.
sentences.grammarNote:
**ما كان قد بدأ** uses the relative/indefinite pronoun ما (that which / what) as the subject of the main clause, followed by the past perfect كان قد + بدأ to express what had originally started; ما as a nominal relative pronoun heading a past perfect clause is a formal MSA rhetorical opening, creating a contrast between the original state and the transformed outcome.
**ينتظره** is the imperfect of Form VIII of نظر (انتظر, to wait for/look forward to), Form VIII here conveying active, eager anticipation rather than passive waiting; the verb takes the resumptive pronoun suffix ـه referring back to the indefinite antecedent شيء, and the whole verb phrase functions as an asyndetic relative clause because شيءٍ is indefinite — the standard MSA rule for relativisation on indefinite heads.
